brand.name: VOLT
brand.mission: To engineer precision tools for people who demand more from their gear

voice.personality: Like a product engineer who knows exactly what the specs mean and why they matter — direct, confident, zero filler
voice.description: VOLT speaks in facts. Every claim is backed by a spec. Every sentence earns its place. We don't persuade — we inform, and let the engineering speak.

general: Lead with specs and measurable performance claims — not feelings
Use active voice exclusively — passive voice signals weak copy
One idea per sentence — clarity over complexity
Never use urgency tactics — the product sells itself
copywriting: Open with the defining spec or performance stat
Follow immediately with the functional benefit that spec delivers
Use 'built for', 'engineered for', 'designed to', 'precision-machined' as framing phrases
Close with a sharp, imperative CTA: 'Order now', 'Get yours', 'Ship it'
Keep descriptions to 60–90 words — every word must justify its presence
No metaphors, no lifestyle narrative, no seasonal framing
support: State the resolution first, then the explanation — never bury the answer
Use numbered steps for processes — no prose where a list works better
Avoid sympathy language that sounds scripted ('We completely understand your frustration')
One acknowledgement sentence maximum, then straight to the fix
Sign off with: 'VOLT Support' — no warm closings, no filler
Reference spec numbers or model codes where relevant for precision
analytics: Lead with the key metric delta in the first sentence — no narrative preamble
Use tables for multi-metric comparisons — prose for single-point insights
State confidence level when using inferred data
Recommendations must include an owner, a timeline, and a measurable outcome
Cut business jargon: no 'synergies', 'leverage', 'action items', 'circle back'
Prioritise by revenue impact — label each recommendation with estimated uplift

copywriting.structure: Key spec or performance headline → Functional benefit (1–2 sentences) → Secondary spec or differentiator (1 sentence) → Sharp imperative CTA
copywriting.headline_style: ALL CAPS or Title Case, spec-first, no flowery punctuation
copywriting.cta_style: Imperative: 'Order now', 'Get yours', 'Ship it today', 'Add to cart'
copywriting.avoid: Flowery prose, emotional hooks, sensory language, lifestyle narrative, seasonal framing

support.greeting: One sentence stating you're addressing the issue — no generic openers
support.resolution_options: Numbered list — solution first, explanation second
support.tone: Direct and informative — no scripted empathy, no filler phrases

analytics.structure: Key metric delta (1 sentence) → Data table or bullet list → Root cause analysis → Recommendations with owner + timeline + target KPI
analytics.language: Plain English, define all acronyms on first use
analytics.focus: Revenue impact, conversion metrics, and performance benchmarks
analytics.flag_assumptions: Label inferred data as 'estimated' and state confidence level
